name: Suffix Array

x.invariant: an array of starting positions of all suffixes of the text, sorted lexicographically; often paired with an LCP array.
operations: build (O(n) with DC3 / SA-IS, or O(n log n))
substring search (O(m log n), or O(m) with LCP)
x.memoryOverhead: ~4–8 bytes per character (one integer per suffix), far less than a suffix tree.
x.locality: Contiguous integer array; far more cache-friendly than a pointer-based suffix tree.
x.mutationModel: Built once over static text; updates require rebuild (or specialised dynamic variants).
x.concurrency: Read-only after build → trivially shareable; parallel construction exists.

def: A sorted array of all suffix positions of a text, giving fast substring and longest-common-substring queries with a fraction of a suffix tree’s memory.
problem: Index a large text for fast substring search and repeat analysis without a suffix tree’s heavy pointer overhead.
mechanism: Sorts all n suffixes lexicographically and stores their start indices. Linear-time builders (SA-IS, DC3) avoid the naive O(n log²n). An auxiliary LCP array accelerates pattern matching and substring statistics.
benefits: Compact (one int per char) vs suffix tree
Foundation for BWT / FM-index full-text search
limits: Some queries need the extra LCP array
Construction is non-trivial to implement well
failures: Naive O(n²log n) build on large texts
Off-by-one in LCP usage corrupts matches
whenNot: Frequently mutating text (use an online index)
myths: "Suffix arrays are weaker than suffix trees" — with LCP they match most queries at lower memory
